title: foundation — data classification taxonomy (THE reference)
description: The 8-tier data classification taxonomy every downstream module's `data-classes.json` references. Foundation defines the tier semantics; downstream modules classify their fields against these tiers. Tiers drive DSAR export scope, right-to-erasure behaviour, encryption-at-rest policy, audit trail depth, and search-index eligibility. Every persistent column in every schema in every module MUST be classified — CI fails on unclassified fields.

public.name: Public
public.definition: Non-identifying, safe to expose in URLs, response bodies, cache keys, log lines. Never subject to erasure or DSAR redaction.
public.encryption: TLS in transit is sufficient. No at-rest encryption required beyond disk-level defaults.
public.dsar: Included in exports. Never subject to erasure.
examples: id, slug, name (of non-personal entities like Tenant, BusinessType, Team)

internal.name: Internal
internal.definition: Platform-internal state. Not personally identifiable. May include operational metadata (rollout flags, feature toggles, denormalised state).
internal.encryption: TLS in transit + disk-level at rest. No column-level encryption.
internal.dsar: Included in exports where relevant to the subject; excluded when it describes system state (e.g. is_system flag).
internal.erasure: Left in place; not personal data.
examples: internal timestamps not tied to a subject (last_check_at, expires_at)

confidential.name: Confidential PII
confidential.definition: Personally identifiable information — email, name, phone, address, IP address, avatar URL, timezone, locale. DSAR-exportable + erasable on subject request.
confidential.encryption: TLS in transit + AES-256 at rest. Encryption keys managed by AWS KMS.
confidential.erasure: Purge-with-audit-record — value replaced with `erased-<hash>@invalid.local` or NULL; audit trail entry retains the original for legal-hold period.
confidential.searchable: Yes, but the search index MUST be pruned on erasure.
confidential.loggable: No — never in application logs. Structured audit logs (via HasAuditable) are allowed with per-tenant retention.
confidential.cacheable: Yes, but cache invalidation triggers on erasure.

restricted.name: Restricted / Credential-Adjacent
restricted.definition: Secrets and credential-adjacent material — password hashes, MFA secrets, API tokens, SSO configuration, integration credentials, verification tokens. NEVER included in DSAR exports.
restricted.encryption: TLS in transit + AES-256-GCM at rest. Encryption keys managed by AWS KMS. Column-level encryption via KMS envelope pattern.
restricted.dsar: NEVER included in DSAR exports (they're secrets, not user data).
restricted.erasure: Crypto-shred — the KMS key wrapping the ciphertext is destroyed. Row remains but ciphertext is unreadable.
restricted.loggable: No, never — the base logger has redaction rules for known restricted field names.
restricted.cacheable: No — every read hits the DB + KMS decrypt.
restricted.auditRule: Every read AND every write is audit-logged with actor + timestamp + reason.
examples: ProviderIdentity.provider_metadata (encrypted OAuth refresh tokens, SAML assertions)
Invitation.token_hash (not encrypted — it's already a one-way hash, but never surfaced)

regulated_minor.name: Regulated Minor
regulated_minor.definition: PII of a data subject under the age of consent (13 in US per COPPA; 13-16 in EU per GDPR Art. 8 depending on member state). Requires verifiable parental consent (VPC) before collection.
regulated_minor.dsar: Included in DSAR exports available to the guardian (not the minor themselves under 13).
regulated_minor.searchable: Restricted — indexing gated by VPC status.
examples: Athlete.date_of_birth when age < 13 (US) or < member-state age (EU)
any per-athlete progress / performance / attendance data for regulated-minor athletes

regulated_health.name: Regulated Health / Safeguarding
regulated_health.definition: Health-related data + safeguarding records (medical clearance, injury reports, safeguarding disclosure log, DBS/WwCC verification status). HIPAA-adjacent in the US; safeguarding statute in UK/AU/IE.
regulated_health.encryption: TLS + AES-256-GCM at rest + FIPS 140-2 Level 2 key material (v2 aspiration).
regulated_health.dsar: Included but with medical-record redaction rules per jurisdiction.
regulated_health.erasure: Anonymise-in-place — medical notes replaced with `[erased at YYYY-MM-DD per data subject request]`, structured fields NULLed.
regulated_health.auditRule: Every read + write audit-logged. Access requires an explicit purpose-of-use annotation.
regulated_health.retentionRule: Safeguarding statute governs — typically 6 years post-last-contact in UK; consult per jurisdiction.
regulated_health.reservedForV2: Not present in v1 schemas. Athletes module will introduce these fields in v2 when the safeguarding vertical lands. HIPAA covered-entity status intentionally NOT pursued in v1.

regulated_financial.name: Regulated Financial
regulated_financial.definition: Payment card data, banking details, tax IDs. PCI-DSS scope. Deliberately kept OUT of our infrastructure — all card data lives in Stripe Elements iframes.
regulated_financial.encryption: N/A — we don't hold this data. Only tokenised references (Stripe customer_id, subscription_id).
regulated_financial.dsar: Tokenised references included; underlying card data is Stripe's DSAR concern.
regulated_financial.erasure: Delete the token; Stripe handles the underlying data per their DPA.
regulated_financial.strategicPosition: Stay SAQ-A. Never accept raw card data.
examples: TenantContact.address for billing purposes (but this is confidential, not regulated_financial — addresses aren't in PCI scope)

audit_log.name: Audit Log
audit_log.definition: Immutable audit records. Records of who did what when, retained for compliance evidence.
audit_log.encryption: TLS + AES-256 at rest. Optional column-level encryption for the diff payload.
audit_log.dsar: Metadata (occurred_at, event, actor_id) included; personal data in the diff payload redacted or anonymised.
audit_log.erasure: Anonymise-not-delete — the audit row is retained (auditors + regulators require the trail) but PII within it is anonymised (ip_address, user_agent, city → NULL; event + occurred_at + actor_type preserved for aggregate).
audit_log.searchable: By occurred_at + event + actor_type only; never by PII content.
audit_log.loggable: The audit rows ARE the log.
audit_log.retentionRule: Regime-specific: SOC 2 wants 1y minimum; UK safeguarding wants 6y; GDPR wants 'no longer than necessary'. Foundation default: P1Y hot + cold-archive to P7Y.

downstreamConvention.eachModuleDeclares: A `data-classes.json` file with a `classes` object mapping each tier name → array of `Model.field` strings.
downstreamConvention.ciRule: Every persistent column in every schema in every module MUST appear in exactly ONE tier in that module's data-classes.json. Unclassified fields fail the build. Fields in multiple tiers fail the build.
